data.title: TKO Group Holdings: Rapidly Expanding The Experience Economy Into 2026
data.description:
Barely 3 months after signing its landmark $7.7 billion, 7-year deal with Paramount, TKO Group Holdings (TKO) announced a 5-year media rights deal between Paramount and Professional Bull Riders (PBR)- a subsidiary of TKO acquired in Q1 2025. Evidently, 2026 will be a momentous year for TKO, as it will kick-start the multi-billion-dollar agreements it has made for key assets, such as UFC, WWE, and PBR (as previously stated). Shares of TKO jumped 56% (YoY) and 27.84% (YTD). The company also recently released its Q3 2025 earnings results, and despite the 27% (YoY) revenue decrease in the quarter, I will discuss why the stock is a hold in view of its robust growth into 2026.

There had been fears that the focus on profits would underscore TKO’s global fan base, especially in properties such as WWE, getting into 2025, especially after it launched on ESPN in September 2025. In its Q3 2025 earnings call, TKO (through its CFO) stated,

Live events and hospitality revenue increased 61% to $83 million. The increase was driven by higher ticket sales revenue, reflecting an increase in average ticket price and total attendance, and an increase in site fee revenue.”

To me, this is a case of TKO adjusting its pricing of its tickets in accordance with the market. WWE’s revenues for the 3 months ending on September 30, 2025, outperformed UFC’s revenue by about $77 million and accounted for nearly 36% of WWE’s overall quarterly revenue. In the 9 months to September 30, 2025, WWE’s revenue grew 22.73% (YoY) to $1.35 billion, with the growth including a jump up in live events. While WWE outperformed UFC and IMG across the board (as far as revenues are concerned), UFC still controls key differentiators, such as marketing and partnerships, where it commands a good lead.

In Q3 2025, UFC’s revenue from partnerships outperformed WWE’s by almost 78%, and it grew 20% (YoY) in the 9 months to September 30, 2025. Essentially, global partnership is a significant revenue driver for TKO as a whole, delivering high margins for the business in the form of recurring multi-year revenues.

We have to keep in mind that UFC is discarding pay-per-view revenues after its $7.7 billion deal, which is supposed to begin in 2026. We also need to consider that UFC 306 broke records in 2024, and total revenues in the 9 months to September 2025 outperformed those of 2025 by 7% (YoY), primarily fueled by, among other things, the sponsorship from Saudi Arabia. There have been momentous investments linked with Saudi Arabia, especially in the global sporting scene, and I believe that this partnership with TKO is a precursor to many more. As a reminder, TKO joined the boxing scene (Zuffa Boxing) in H1 2025 following its partnership with Saudi company, General Entertainment Authority, whose launch is expected in January 2026 and is set to be integrated with a prediction feature.

TKO is Entering the Prediction Market Zone

TKO recently announced that it had struck a multi-year deal with global prediction market company, Polymarket, for UFC and Zuffa Boxing. Under the agreement, the two entities will “integrate the prediction market technology directly into the live fan experience.”

The whole point is to grow fan engagement in both UFC and Zuffa boxing through technology. While making this announcement, TKO explained that this agreement will allow live fan predictions to be featured (on both platforms) while the fights are still in progress. Unlike other bookies that integrate sportsbook/betting odds (or probable outcomes), it will feature probabilities implied by the fan base (embedded as a scoreboard). For Polymarket, this deal is an expansion from its traditional politics and global trends to live sporting, especially after landing a partnership agreement with the National Hockey League (NHL). For the NHL, this deal means higher brand exposure and a robust fan engagement platform since the 2025/26 season started in early October 2025. However, TKO is integrating Polymarket into its live events, making it very unique. Then again, when we consider the demographics of TKO’s fan base, which mainly consists of the youth.

According to research, the push by tech giants has forced sports broadcasters to take up mobile-centric approaches that not only appeal to the youth, especially Gen Zs, but are also on demand. There is the aspect of diversity and the dual-screen viewership (watching live events while engaging other fans), features that will be central to Polymarket’s dominance at TKO.

As of 2024, the decentralized prediction market (which houses Polymarket) was valued at $1.4 billion and is expected to reach $95.5 billion by 2035, growing at a CAGR of 46.8%.

Valuation

To evaluate the company, I will use the enterprise value (EV) to sales (EV/S) metric, as it will include cash and the company’s debt in the calculation. TKO’s forward EV/sales ratio stands at 4.95X against the sector median of 1.92X (a difference of 157%). For its FY 2025 revenue, the company gave guidance ranging from $4.69 billion to $4.72 billion (a midpoint of $4.705 billion). To calculate the EV, I will multiply 4.95X by $4.705 billion, giving us $23.3 billion. I will calculate TKO’s market capitalization by subtracting debt ($3.7 billion) from the EV and adding cash ($861.41 million), giving us $20.45 billion. TKO’s total shares outstanding as of October 31, 2025, were 78.922 million. To get the future price, I will divide the market cap by the outstanding share balance, which gives $259.20. As of this writing, TKO’s share price was $183.03, meaning that the stock is undervalued by 41.62% (YoY).

I will compare the performance of the stock with some of its peers.

Of the stocks mentioned in the tabulated analysis, only TKO has a possible significant upside of +40% (YoY). It is also the only company offering a dividend at a yield of 1.65%.

To further substantiate this, TKO has a share repurchase program dubbed the ASR Agreement that has since repurchased about $800 million worth of shares, translating to about 4.22 million shares (as at November 17, 2025). Under the Agreement is a share trading plan, "The 10b5-1 Plan" that seeks to repurchase an additional $174 million worth of shares by February 2026. The two plans will likely see TKO repurchase more than 5 million shares into 2026, driving up the price to its target.

WBD was the highest mover, rising 141% (YoY) and outperforming the S&P 500 by more than 127%.

Risks

I have pegged my analysis on the view that TKO will realize its sales guidance range of $4.69 billion to $4.72 billion. As of Q3 2025, TKO reported a 27% (YoY) revenue decline, meaning that continual decline in sales into Q4 2025 will adversely affect the stock price.

Will TKO Meet its guidance?

I believe that TKO's revenue guidance of $4.69 billion to $4.72 billion is not too far-fetched, seeing that the guidance as of Q1 2025, stood at the range of $2.93bn to $3.075bn. The midpoint of $3 billion means that the company is forecasting an increase of 56.67% (YoY). At the time (Q1 2025), the company was mainly looking at two strongholds, UFC and WWE, but there are now PBR and Zuffa Boxing. If we were to also consider emerging growth factors, then TKO is at a fair position. For instance, TKO's talent and marketing arm, International Management Group's (IMG's), revenue in the 9 months to September 30, 2025, exceeded that of UFC by 1.7% at about $1.2 billion.

Further, TKO is set to reap from its integration of Polymarket into its live shows. Fans are set to increase their interaction with the fighters, which is a significant expansion of its experience economy, as indicated earlier. The audience will be using crypto bets to predict the outcome of matches- and this exposure cannot be downplayed. Polymarket will also act as Zuffa Boxing's main brand partner and, at the same time, create a prediction market for the fans to bet. TKO will, therefore, grow their earnings from ticket sales to market predictions in their live events.

Bottom Line

I have rated TKO Group as a hold despite its revenue decline in Q3 2025 due to its expected growth and expansion of its experience economy. The company is big on global partnerships that are profitable to the company, and the recent deal with Polymarket will improve customer engagement and increase viewership. I have also established that TKO is undervalued by at least 40% (YoY), with most of its projects expected to come online in 2026.

data.title: LPL Financial: The Bulls Could Keep Charging As Client Assets Keep Growing
data.description:
Today's Pick: A Financial Firm With An Earnings Beat

Taking a break from REITs this week, which I've said much about lately, today I'm turning my attention to a financial firm that had its last earnings results on Oct. 30th, LPL Financial (LPLA), when it beat EPS estimates by an impressive $0.71.

According to its profile, the firm "provides an integrated platform of brokerage and investment advisory services to independent financial advisors and financial advisors at institutions in the United States."

My Rating: Buy

My thesis today argues that even though LPLA is trading at a premium and appears overvalued and also shows much weaker margins than key peers, what pushed the needle into the bullish column is the proven momentum against the S&P 500 as a benchmark, company-specific growth levers like new client money inflows and new businesses/portfolios acquired, and an impressive credit rating from 3 major agencies.

My worksheet below summarizes the 7 categories I evaluated and how they contributed to the average score, which led to a buy rating:

My followers on this site may notice that I have added some new items, like considering a stock's longer-term performance against a major benchmark, but also separating the discussion about the overall sector and the company itself, which I think readers will appreciate.

Long-Term Return vs. S&P 500: A Proven Outperformer Among Key Peers

As a new feature for my readers this fall, I'm taking a closer look at a stock's comparison performance vs a major benchmark.

From momentum data, here is a 10-year comparison of this stock's price return vs. the S&P 500, along with 4 of its peers I selected in the investment banking/brokerage niche of financials:

In this comparison, LPLA achieved a +731% price performance over a 10-year period vs the S&P 500 of just +222%. None of the other peers in this group, including Morgan Stanley (MS), Nomura (NMR), Raymond James (RJF), and Stifel (SF), came close to outperforming this stock, as the chart shows.

While this may be based on historic data, I include it as it gives me a framework for whether this stock has proven itself over a longer period in outperforming not only a major index but also similar peers, and it has.

Sector Risk: Rising Advisor Demand Also Facing AI and Sector Competition

Here, let's consider sector risk and opportunities looking ahead.

Within the overall financials sector, LPLA is in a specific niche, focusing heavily on wealth management/advisory. In fact, in its Q3 presentation (pg 5), it boasted of having 32,100 advisors and serving $2.3T in assets.

What comes to mind when thinking about this niche is whether there could be continued market demand for specialized advisory, but also thinking about the considerable competition from big financial brands offering similar services.

I would point to research from global consultancy McKinsey that said last February of the wealth management industry that "demand for its services continues to grow as Americans become wealthier and their needs become more complex," but also point out a supply/demand imbalance:

We estimate that by 2034, at current advisor productivity levels, the advisor workforce will decline to the point where the industry faces a shortage of roughly 100,000 advisors.

So, I think that firms such as LPLA could see continued upside as they try to fill that market demand well into the future. From their own outlook (investor presentation, pg 7), we can see they perceive a "growing demand for advice" well into 2027/beyond:

Additionally, Seeking Alpha growth data shows this entire peer group I selected having a positive forward revenue growth expected, with LPLA the 2nd highest in this group:

However, investors should consider that a handful of megacap financial brands have considerable dominance in the wealth space, and one look at the 2025 Top Private Wealth Management Teams by Barron's shows that the top 20 are dominated by advisors affiliated with firms like Morgan Stanley, UBS (UBS), Bank of America's Merrill Lynch, and Ameriprise (AMP), for instance.

The other question is whether the surge of AI (artificial intelligence) usage and proliferation will be complementary to the role of advisors, or could it pose a future risk of replacing many of the junior ones? Interestingly, a 2024 study by MIT Sloan School of Management said that AI tools using LLMs (large language models) could become more advanced and personalized with time:

As they develop the ability to “talk” to both an elderly retiree who never finished high school and a professional regulator, LLMs will not just be able to answer questions satisfactorily but do so in a relatable tone, the way a human financial adviser would.

Thus, the sector risk paints a mixed picture for LPLA, with potential demand for advisory on the rise, but also the headwinds from sector competition and future AI advancements, hence justifying an optimistic neutral stance in this category.

Company Growth: Net New Assets And Portfolio Growth, On Top of Proven Fees Growth

Next, I want to focus on company-specific growth factors.

We can see from the income statement that LPLA makes its money from asset management fees, underwriting/investment banking fees, and, to a much lesser extent, income from interest/dividends.

Company growth, therefore, could come from recent growth in new client monies /client inflows since this can drive future fees revenue if thinking like a future-minded investor and analyst. It can also come from acquiring other businesses and their portfolios. When it comes to growth in assets under management, which drives fees, that can be inorganic via appreciating market values or organic via net new client assets coming to the firm to manage.

Notably on that front, Q3 results reported "net new assets were $308 billion," adding that "this included $275 billion of acquired net new assets resulting from the acquisition of Commonwealth." So, the actual organic growth would be the new assets that are not acquisition-related.

In terms of longer trends, we can see from the Q3 investor deck (pg 6) that "organic" growth has generally been on a positive track record since 2020:

From another angle, in that same investor deck we can see how organic growth has been achieved across both the Advisory and Brokerage segments, which also points to the firm having revenue diversification:

Further notables from that release were that the firm "onboarded First Horizon with $18B of brokerage and advisory assets, of which $17B transitioned onto our platform in Q3."

If you care about trends, consider from Q3 Key Metrics that LPLA has seen a positive growth trend in fees in the period from Q3 2022 to Q3 2025, across multiple fee sources, which I think helps boost revenue diversification:

So, the growth story here is positive, as net new assets won can drive future fees as well as new acquisitions, and this firm already has a proven track record when it comes to fee growth trends, supporting a bullish stance in this category.

Profit Margins: Improvement Opportunity Awaits vs Key Peers, As FY25 EPS Could Decline

Moving on, let's consider the role of profit margins and how they can impact profitability.

From the peer comparison below, a downside risk that sticks out is that LPLA has a net margin of just 5.4%, well below all of the peers in this group, with Morgan Stanley particularly dominant with a nearly +24% margin:

Such data points to LPLA being considerably less efficient at generating profits from revenue, than these key peers with some similar business segments like wealth advisory and investment banking, so it makes LPLA less competitive, I think.

To be fair, though, LPLA does show signs of being committed to boosting efficiency, as it said in its investor presentation, and I will be eager to learn over the next year what the firm has done, for example, to make use of AI to drive cost efficiency/productivity.

I think it will also be critical for management to address the following declining trend in the operating margin since Jan. 2023, as this can weigh down on investor sentiment:

So, based on the multiple points above, I am inclined to give it a very low hold rating in this category, as it needs considerable margin improvement within this peer group, not to mention that the analyst consensus forecasts a -16% YoY EPS decline this year, but then in FY2026 there is expected to be a +58% recovery, so it justifies a longer-term view of this stock rather than near-term trading.

Dividend Growth & Coverage: Capital Reinvestment Has Been Multi-Faceted

As this is a dividend stock, let's talk next about its dividend growth history and potential to cover its dividend going forward.

We can see from my peer comparison below that LPLA was the worst dividend grower in this group, when considering the time period of 2016 - 2025, while RJF and Nomura saw considerable dividend growth in that same period:

I think this could matter to dividend income investors looking to pick a stock that has proven itself as a dividend grower over a longer period, showing that its business model can be sustainable and support dividend payouts and growth.

At first glance, if only going by the chart above, one would assume this firm is struggling to grow dividends. However, researching further, this time in the Q3 presentation(pg 22), we can see that this firm has also been allocating capital to acquisitions and organic growth as well as share buybacks rather than just dividend payouts, so holistically I think that should be considered as well since those can all benefit shareholders ultimately.

Further, when it comes to cash flow growth trends, a fundamental metric like operating cashflow growth shows a nearly +52% expected forward growth, according to Seeking Alpha data, and that core operating cash should help support all of these other strategies including the dividend.

With that said, I am cautiously bullish in this category.

Balance Sheet Risk: A Favorable Outlook by Fitch and Modest Debt-To-Equity vs Peers

Next, let's consider balance sheet risk, as this goes beyond just revenue and profits but I think is part of a fundamental look into this firm as a business.

The debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio of LPLA is 1.61, modest when compared to this peer group, and puts this firm somewhere in the low/middle bracket of leverage risk in comparison to equity:

In addition, I would point out the firm's impressive credit ratings (as per investor deck, pg. 23) which show 3 major agencies favoring this firm:

I think that is excellent news and should add to some investor confidence. To put some of this into perspective, from the rating note by Fitch this summer, the agency said:

The Stable Outlook reflects Fitch's expectation that LPLA will maintain prudent capital management, with Fitch-calculated leverage near 2.5x and interest coverage above 6x over the Outlook horizon. Fitch also expects LPLA to sustain strong operating performance and sound liquidity.

So, I believe the balance sheet risk story adds more to the bullish side of this stock, since I think an investment-grade firm with modest D/E could be in a more favorable position if it needs future debt raises.

Share Price Growth Potential: Trading At Premium And Overvaluation, Yet Wall St Expects More Upside

Coming close to wrapping up this discussion today, we cannot ignore the role that share price and valuation play in investment decisions.

When it comes to the share price, to add some technical analysis to the story as well, my chart below supports a bullish stance if one were to use the concept of death crosses and golden crosses. In this example, a death cross occurred in early October, and now it seems the trend is approaching what could be a golden cross again (50-day SMA crossing above the 200-day SMA), indicating a buy signal. I indicated these indicators in green and red below:

However, beyond technicals, we should also consider valuation, so I did the following comparison with its 4 similar peers from earlier:

From this comparison, LPLA is indeed overvalued compared to peer averages. This worksheet uses non-GAAP forward P/E, and trailing price-to-book, since this firm's GAAP data is impacted by one-time items like acquisition costs, which can skew the valuation multiple. What this worksheet tells us is that LPLA is considerably overvalued compared to this peer group average on both multiples. For instance, its price-to-book is 175% above this peer group average P/B.

We can also see from balance sheet data that its book value per share is a mere $63, yet its stock is trading at such elevated multiples to its book value. So, the market is eager to pay a premium for this stock, it seems, above book value.

On the one hand, it seems like the market bullishness/exuberance on this stock has some justification: new acquisitions, new client monies, strong credit ratings, and FY26 expected EPS growth. On the other hand, mergers and integrations do not always go smoothly, and there are integration issues that can arise. Further, the optimistic FY26 EPS growth consensus could also disappoint.

For these reasons and the mixed picture, let's consider the following worksheet where I compare my future price target with that of the Wall Street consensus:

From my worksheet above (which is based on Monday's numbers, by the way, as of the writing of this article), my own target (which uses fwd P/E x EPS consensus) implies a -4% downside, but Wall St who likely uses other models, has a target implying a +23% upside to this stock.

Again, much like earlier, the multiple points I raised above paints a mixed picture: technical signals pointing to a bullish case but valuations exceeding peer averages, and my own price target implying some downside potential while Wall St expects further upside. I also still remain somewhat concerned about its weaker margins vs peers, for instance, which I raised earlier.

Ultimately, despite this neutral/mixed picture, this section did not bring down the overall buy rating today.

Challenge to My Rating: Extended Market Declines

A challenge to my bullish case that could transpire, and not mentioned elsewhere in this rating score, is that this firm's AUM and its success is tied considerably to rising or falling markets, so an extended period of falling equity markets could impact the values of equity assets under management, and hence could impact a firm's fees.

This is considerable in a firm very dependent on fees and less so on interest income from loans like a traditional bank.

A mitigating factor could be a period of slow but sustainable market growth over the next decade. The good news is that a major forecast from Morgan Stanley, reported this week by Reuters, "forecast the S&P 500 index to touch 7,800 by the end of 2026, about a 16% upside from current levels, amid robust earnings growth and AI-driven efficiency gains."

I think if that forecast is met or exceeded, it could be favorable for firms like LPLA, but a miss could be a downside risk to consider, making my view today overly optimistic.

Conclusion: Agree With Wall St and SA Analysts

To conclude, I reiterate my buy rating on this stock, and it agrees with today's buy consensus from SA analysts and Wall St.

My call to action for investors in this case would be that although this stock may appear very expensive if one were to only consider price, in a longer-term sense there appears to be some upside potential to grab even at these prices, and I have shown what is driving this despite the risks also discussed.

data.title: Genesco Is Cheap, But Structural Problems Remain
data.description:
Back in late December of last year, I made the decision to downgrade Genesco (GCO) from a ‘hold’ to a ‘sell.’ The fact of the matter is that the company had been contending with worsening fundamentals, with some of that being structural in nature. Although digital sales were growing, and even though management was cutting costs, the structural issue involved a decline in store count. At the end of the day, I argued that this would ultimately make it a disappointing prospect that would carry too much risk. Since then, we have seen some positive results on the revenue side. But still, bottom-line pressures persist. When you add on top of this the continued decline in store count, not even the fact that shares are incredibly cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to some other similar firms, justifies a bullish outlook. In fact, until we can see some light at the end of the tunnel, I think that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate is the right choice.

Taking a look at Genesco

In case you are new to Genesco, the company operates as a retailer in the footwear and apparel market. Major brands that it owns include Journeys, Journeys Kids, Little Burgundy, Johnston & Murphy, and others. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the company for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. Revenue during this time actually ended up being range-bound. And a good chunk of this was because of robust digital sales. In 2024, for instance, e-commerce revenue jumped by 8%. And in 2025, even as same-store sales were flat, total comparable sales grew by 3% thanks to a 12% jump in e-commerce revenue.

As great as the e-commerce revenue situation is, however, the structural problem is that the store count for the company continues to decline. It dropped from 1,410 locations in 2023 down to 1,278 by the end of last year. And by the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, that number had decreased even more to 1,253. Over the last three years, net profits have gone from $71.9 million to negative $18.9 million. Both adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA have also declined, though cost-cutting measures did improve results for 2025 compared to 2024 in this regard.

I wish I could say that fiscal 2026 was looking up. In one way, it is. But in another it's not. During the first half of the year, the company reported revenue of $1.02 billion. As comparable sales grew by 5%, driven once again by strong e-commerce revenue, revenue expanded compared to the $982.8 million reported a year earlier. The star of this show was undoubtedly its Journeys Group segment, which saw revenue jump 5.8% from $558.3 million to $590.8 million. Management said that this was thanks to a 9% pop in comparable sales even as the firm suffered from a 5% decrease associated with a reduction in store count. Both conversion and transaction size can be thanked for the improvement in revenue that the company saw here.

Management attributed this to certain transformational initiatives that are finally starting to gain traction. One example would be the firm’s 4.0 remodels of its Journeys stores, which have adopted modular and flexible designs, improved visuals, storytelling opportunities, increased digital integration, and that have emphasized a focus on footwear. The company also mentioned that the launch of a signature new footwear partnership with Wrangler that should occur next fall should also help to propel revenue higher moving forward. And on top of this, continuing product innovation is helping to keep the brand fresh, especially when it comes to the teen audience that it serves.

This was not the only area of growth for the company. The Schuh Group segment reported a 2.6% increase in revenue from $216.9 million to $222.5 million. Management said that this was because of foreign currency fluctuations; however, online sales growth did help. While acknowledging that conditions in the UK remain challenged, it is clear that the business is holding up well. And on top of this, we saw a bit of growth when it came to the Genesco Brands Group, with revenue popping 6.3% from $57.3 million to $61 million.

Greater sales of Levi’s and private label footwear helped the business, though it did see some decline elsewhere, such as with its Dockers product line. The only pain point that the company saw involved its Johnston & Murphy Group segment, with revenue contracting 3.1% from $150.2 million to $145.6 million. Management attributed this not only to lower same-store sales and a 3% reduction in the number of stores in operation, but also to lower wholesale revenue.

I would be tempted to upgrade the company to a ‘hold’ right now based on the revenue increase, the initiatives that management is undertaking, and the fact that shares are so cheap, as you will see shortly. But the one thing that's stopping me is that bottom line results continue to worsen. The firm went from a net loss of $34.2 million in the first half of 2025 to a net loss of $39.7 million at the same time this year. Multiple operating segments performed poorly on this front. For instance, the Schuh Group segment reported a decline in operating income from $1.4 million to negative $6.1 million. Lower gross margins caused by increased promotional activity, as well as changes in brand mix and higher selling and administrative costs associated with the deleverage of expenses as store count declines, all hurt the segment. We also saw the Johnston & Murphy Group segment go from a profit of $2 million to a loss of $1.3 million. The same exact issues affected this unit. And even though the Genesco Brands Group segment remained profitable, it reported a decline in profits from $1.7 million to just under $1.4 million. Higher closeout sales affected the business as it exited some of its licenses, plus tariffs played an unfavorable role here (as they did for other segments as well).

Other profitability metrics for the company worsened also. Operating cash flow went from negative $6 million to negative $14.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a worsening from $0.9 million to negative $5.7 million. And to top things off, EBITDA went from negative $12.9 million to negative $15.3 million. Management has said that overall results this year probably will be better than expected in some respects. For instance, revenue is now expected to grow by between 3% and 4%, compared to the 1% to 2% increase previously anticipated. Comparable sales should now climb by between 4% and 5% compared to the 2% to 3% range forecasted before. However, certain expenses will be worse, specifically with the gross margin of the company now falling by 50 to 60 basis points compared to the 20 to 30 basis point drop previously anticipated.

If we project out results this year, we would likely get adjusted operating cash flow of about $68.4 million and EBITDA of $80.9 million. This is based on management's estimates combined with some of my own assumptions. Using these estimates, as well as the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are very low trading multiples, I would normally entice me. But the way I see it, until we see some stabilization in the store count, there is just too much risk here. That's why, even though the stock is cheap compared to most other similar firms as shown in the table below, it is difficult for me to pull the trigger on an upgrade just yet, especially at a time when I believe that the US is heading toward a recession.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Genesco 4.9 4.5
Citi Trends (CTRN) 107.0 94.9
Zumiez (ZUMZ) 14.7 17.8
Caleres (CAL) 11.9 6.6
J.Jill (JILL) 4.4 4.6
Designer Brands (DBI) 3.2 16.0

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that Genesco makes for a very interesting prospect. However, I'm not ready to turn neutral or bullish on it just yet. I am encouraged by some of the progress management has achieved. But between broader economic concerns and worries about what I still consider to be structural issues for the business, I think that maintaining it as a soft ‘sell’ makes sense even though the stock has fallen 20.5% since I downgraded it to that.

data.title: Seix High Yield Bond SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

As we enter the final quarter of the year, the high yield market shows healthy fundamentals, generally supportive technicals, and valuations that still offer reasonable yields for risk-adjusted income generation, in our view. While the economy seems to be softening, it has remained resilient in the face of sticky yet seemingly contained inflation. Third quarter bank earnings indicated that consumers remained generally healthy, even as some got squeezed by inflationary pressures and broader data showed some signs of softening. As we move forward, risk assets may find support from large fiscal deficits and a Federal Reserve (Fed) that is cutting rates. Furthermore, it seems probable that the next Chairman of the Fed will have a bias for further rate cuts. Having said that, the bankruptcies of First Brands and TriColor Holdings, as well as recent disclosures from Zions Bancorp and Western Alliance Bancorp, show the results of lax underwriting. At this point, we believe these events are idiosyncratic in nature but are monitoring the situation closely. For now, the market is mostly overlooking the government shutdown and rising trade tensions with China.

Within high yield, the fundamentals have been gradually softening, a trend that has been observed over previous quarters. However, overall credit metrics remain healthy. Earnings growth has gained momentum, although leverage ratios have deteriorated as debt levels have risen. According to an analysis by J.P. Morgan, second quarter revenue for high yield issuers increased by 2.1% year-over-year and EBITDA increased by 1.1% year-over-year. As we have noted in previous quarters, the fundamentals for high-quality high yield issuers have been more favorable. Year-over-year, median EBITDA for BBs increased by 2.3%, Bs increased by 0.1%, and CCCs declined by 3.5%. Second quarter earnings were generally favorable relative to expectations for high yield issuers. Furthermore, guidance was generally positive, and the negative impacts of tariffs and inflation have not been a large driver of earnings disappointments.

This positive fundamental backdrop has contributed to default rates that have declined slightly and are running well below long-term averages. The high yield default rate is currently at 1.4% compared to a long-term average of 3.3%.

During the quarter, BBs widened out slightly by 6 basis points (BPs) as lower quality credits caught a bid, with Bs and CCCs tightening 22 bps and 79 bps, respectively. While the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index (H0A0) increased by 2.41% in 3Q, the performance of the ratings groupings in the index are as follows: the ICE BofA BB U.S. High Yield Index (H0A1) increased by 2.17%; the ICE BofA Single-B U.S. High Yield Index (H0A2) increased by 2.43%; and the ICE BofA CCC & Lower U.S. High Yield Index (H0A3) increased by 3.35%. Lower-quality credits continued to outperform from 2Q into 3Q as the prospect of lower yields and solid economic growth created a tailwind that assisted their bottom line.

The option adjusted spread (OAS) for the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index began the year at 292 bps, widened to 461 bps on April 7th shortly after the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, and ended 3Q at 280 bps.

Performance

The Seix High Yield Bond SMA that we manage increased by 1.66% (gross) in 3Q25 (1.03% net). This performance was 75 bps fewer than the benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 2.41%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included technology, chemicals, and consumer products/ textile/tobacco. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, energy, and media non-cable.

The portfolio increased by 5.93% (gross) for the year-to-date period (3.97% net). This performance was 116 bps less than the benchmark, ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 7.09%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included chemicals, technology, and restaurants/food/beverage/supermarkets. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, telecommunications, and media non-cable.

Our longer-term objective is to provide a smoother ride with outperformance through the full credit cycle and the strongest relative performance generated during periods of dislocation when the market is offering a plethora of attractively priced securities.

Current Strategy & Outlook

As we enter the final quarter of 2025, our strategy remains grounded in disciplined credit analysis and a focus on the higher-quality segments of the high yield market. The macroeconomic backdrop continues to be defined by elevated policy uncertainty, slowing growth tempered by economic resilience, and sticky inflation. These conditions, combined with valuations that are at the tighter end of their historical range, reinforce our up-in-quality bias. This is further supported by the relative strength of BB rated issuers, which have demonstrated more stable earnings and greater financial flexibility, while CCC rated issuers remain under fundamental pressure, with compressed margins and more constrained access to the capital markets. As a result, we remain underweight CCCs.

Fundamentals for high yield issuers remain strong despite the recent trend of modest credit metric erosion. Second quarter earnings and guidance were generally better than expected, with revenue and EBITDA exhibiting positive year-over-year performance. Nevertheless, in aggregate, high yield issuers saw modest deterioration in leverage, interest coverage, and margins. Leverage for high yield issuers increased by 0.14x to 4.2x (slightly better than the historical average of 4.29x), interest coverage was unchanged at 4.71x (above the long-term average of 4.51x), and margins deteriorated modestly to 14.2% (a four-year low). We believe solid fundamentals combined with yields that are still reasonably attractive have contributed to a solid technical backdrop for the high-quality part of the high yield market.

data.title: Global Farm Machinery Makers Face Another Tough Year, But A Turn May Be Coming
data.description:
The world’s leading farm machinery makers are facing a prolonged slowdown as weakening farmer sentiment, falling crop prices, trade uncertainty, and higher borrowing costs weigh on demand for agricultural equipment.

According to Visible Alpha consensus estimates, combined revenues of major global manufacturers — including Deere & Co. (DE), AGCO Corp. (AGCO), and Valmont Industries Inc. (VMI) in the US; Ag Growth International Inc. (AFN) (OTCPK:AGGZF)(AFN:CA) in Canada; CNH Industrial NV (CNH) and Bucher Industries AG (BUCN)(OTCPK:BCHHF) in Europe; Kubota Corp. (6326)(OTCPK:KUBTF) in Japan; and China’s Zoomlion Heavy Industry Science and Technology Co. Ltd. (000157)(OTCPK:ZLIOF) — are expected to decline again in 2025, albeit at a slower pace than in 2024.

For most of these companies, agriculture equipment is the core business. Exceptions include Bucher Industries, Valmont Industries, and Zoomlion, where the segment accounts for roughly 38%, 25%, and 10% of total revenues, respectively.

2025: Another down year, but less severe

Across this group, total revenue is projected to fall 8% year-on-year in 2025, following a sharper 15% slump in 2024. Agricultural machinery sales remain the pressure point: the segment is forecast to decline 11% in 2025 after a 19% slide in 2024, reflecting continued weakness in key end-markets.

The steepest agricultural machinery revenue declines are expected at:

  • Deere: –15% to $27 billion
  • CNH Industrial: –12% to $12.3 billion
  • AGCO: –16% to $9.8 billion

Other manufacturers are expected to post smaller, single-digit declines. The notable outlier is China’s Zoomlion, where analysts expect agriculture machinery revenue to rise 8% to $704 million, supported by stable domestic demand and policy-led infrastructure investment.

Earnings pressure intensifies

The downcycle is even more visible in bottom-line expectations. Net income is forecast to fall sharply at several major manufacturers:

  • Deere: –30% to $5 billion
  • Kubota: –31% to $1 billion
  • CNH Industrial: –55% to $593 million
  • AGCO: –34% to $370 million
  • Bucher: –10% to $257 million
  • Ag Growth: –45% to $37 million

By contrast, two companies are positioned to grow earnings despite the broader downturn:

  • Valmont Industries: +10% to $383 million, supported by steady demand for irrigation systems
  • Zoomlion: +51% to $595 million, driven by strong exports and domestic policy support

A bottom in sight?

While 2025 forecasts remain subdued, analysts generally view the year as the bottom of the current cycle. From 2026 onward, Visible Alpha consensus points to gradual improvement across revenue, machinery sales, and profitability.

A rebound in commodity prices, clearer trade policy, and easing borrowing costs could help revive farm capital spending, providing a potential recovery path for the leading machinery makers after two years of contraction.

data.title: Aptera Motors: Another Chance To Sell
data.description:
On Monday morning before the market opened, we received third quarter results from Aptera Motors (SEV). The company, which is looking to launch a solar-powered electric vehicle, is still far away from generating any actual revenues. With another quarter of losses and cash burn in the books, investors today are likely facing a massive amount of dilution moving forward as the company's financial picture remains quite troubled.

Previous coverage of the name

It was a little more than a month ago that I previously looked at Aptera, right after the company went public via a direct listing. At that time, I noted how the company was going to need a massive amount of capital to get its concept vehicle to actual production, with large losses and cash burn pressuring the balance sheet. Since that article, shares have lost more than 16%, while the S&P 500 has risen by almost 1.5%.

A look at the Q3 results

There is still no concrete timeline for when Aptera will start to generate any actual revenue. At the moment, the company is working on cost discipline and capital raising activities to support production validation. In Q3, the GAAP net loss was roughly $5.5 million, an improvement from the $8.2 million seen a year earlier. On an adjusted basis, the $2.9 million loss was basically half of the red amount seen in Q3 2024.

At the end of September, the company had roughly $12 million in cash and equivalents on its balance sheet, while being debt-free. That number was down about a million dollars from the end of Q2, as a few million in cash burn was offset by $2 million in grant funds received from the California Energy Commission. The company also has a $75 million equity credit line in place through which it can raise capital. In October, the company filed a registration statement for 6 million shares related to this share purchase agreement.

The outstanding share count is on the rise a bit currently. At the end of Q3, there were 9.28 million Class B shares outstanding, which are the ones that trade in the market. There were also another 18 million-plus Class A shares outstanding, which can be converted to Class B. As the company's 10-Q filing states, the Class B count as of November 14th was up to nearly 10.16 million. That number could rise quite substantially moving forward, between a combination of Class A conversions as well as equity sales to raise capital.

The current valuation picture

As of Wednesday's close, Aptera shares were trading at $6.69 each. If you combine the Class A and B outstanding share counts, you get a little more than 27.37 million total shares. That gives the company a current valuation of roughly $183 million at this point. Normally, I'd use price to sales to value the name, like I do with any electric vehicle makers that are consistent money losers, but the company hasn't generated any revenue yet.

EV leader Tesla (TSLA) goes for a low double-digit times its annual sales currently, while most other names go in the low single digits, well under 2 times their annual revenues. For Aptera to justify its current valuation, it needs to do quite a bit of revenue, and that's not likely coming until 2027 at the earliest. Because it just went public and is still rather small currently, there are no major Wall Street analysts covering the name yet.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Aptera reported its first set of quarterly results since going public on Monday, and the numbers were about as expected. The business is still several quarters away from generating any revenue, and management is working to keep operating expenses as low as possible for now to keep cash burn at a minimum. The company still needs to raise quite a bit of funding to get its solar electric vehicle to market, which means current investors will be diluted quite a bit moving forward.

With another quarter in the books and the balance sheet slightly weaker, I have to reiterate a sell on Aptera for the time being. With the company likely needing well over $100 million more before deliveries even start, the stock probably will face a ton of pressure as equity capital is raised. It would not surprise me if the outstanding share count more than doubles over the next year. Right now, there are so many electric vehicle companies out there, so even though Aptera is a bit different, it's got a long way to go to prove itself.

data.title: U.S. Managed Care Q3 2025 Earnings Recap: Costs Grow As ACA Worries Loom
data.description:
US managed care insurers' third quarter earnings season was characterized by higher medical costs amid political uncertainty surrounding extensions of Affordable Care Act tax credits.

Many of the top publicly traded US managed care insurers' medical costs grew during the third quarter, resulting in mixed responses from investors even as revenues grew year over year and earnings per share remained largely stable.

A common issue affecting the sector's third quarter earnings calls was ongoing cost pressure, primarily related to government-subsidized Medicaid and Medicare Advantage plans.

Costs still on the rise

After a challenging 2025 for industry leader UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH), the managed care insurer again highlighted the high costs associated with its Medicaid plans, which experienced heightened acuity levels since state-led procedural disenrollment resumed in March 2023 following the COVID-19 pandemic.

The path to Medicaid recovery will be challenging, as states have not funded the program in line with actual cost trends, according to comments on an Oct. 28 earnings call by Tim Noel, CEO of UnitedHealthcare, UnitedHealth's insurance arm.

"While we're making steady progress in bridging this gap with states, the mismatch between rate adequacy and member acuity will likely extend through 2026," Noel said.

Similarly, Molina Healthcare Inc. (MOH) CEO Joseph Zubretsky lamented a "very challenging medical cost environment" during an earnings call and said that Medicaid rates may not be adequate.

"Our early 2025 rate increases were sufficient at the beginning of the year, but as medical cost trends increased beyond those rates, our [medical cost ratio increased each quarter]," Zubretsky said. "The rate updates we received later in the year and risk corridors did not provide an adequate buffer."

Molina's Medicaid medical cost ratio reached 92% during the quarter, according to Zubretsky, while its senior-aimed Medicare plan hit 93.6% as high levels of utilization continued. However, even Molina's marketplace plans were not immune to rising costs, as plans purchased through state exchanges had a medical cost ratio of 95.6%, significantly higher than expected, according to the CEO.

Although Humana Inc. (HUM) maintained its full-year EPS estimate, CEO James Rechtin acknowledged during an earnings call that the company's Medicare Advantage Stars ratings were "disappointing."

"We are disappointed, but we are not surprised by our bonus year '27 Stars results," Rechtin said. "The results are consistent with our baseline planning scenario, and our outlook remains the same as we previously communicated at our investor conference in June."

Humana previously took issue with how the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services calculates its Star ratings, filing its first lawsuit against the body in October 2024, which was ultimately dismissed a year later. The government body annually issues quality ratings of Medicare Advantage offerings, which impact bonus payments insurers receive from the US government.

In Humana's current measurement year, bonus year 2028, the company is seeing meaningful year-over-year improvement across most metrics and is on the right track, Rechtin said.

ACA credits to lapse

Rate issues are not limited to Medicaid, as UnitedHealth joined a growing chorus of insurers expecting to be affected by changes in the Affordable Care Act markets.

ACA plans, which are offered through state-based marketplaces and directly from insurers rather than through an employer, have been under scrutiny as the extension of tax credits to lower plan costs has become a key issue for the recent federal government shutdown. While chatter remains of possible legislative solutions, several Democratic senators relented and ended the shutdown.

Prior to the shutdown, most insurers had already priced in the expectation that the tax credits would not be extended.

UnitedHealth's insurance arm submitted rate filings in nearly all of the 30 states where it offers ACA plans, reflecting an average rate increase of over 25%, according to Noel. Consequently, the company expects its ACA membership to drop significantly, Noel said.

"Where we are unable to reach agreement on sustainable rates, we are enacting targeted service area reductions," Noel said. "We believe these actions will establish a sustainable premium base, while likely reducing our ACA enrollment by approximately two-thirds."

Despite these challenges, analysts at J.P. Morgan ranked the insurer's stock "overweight," noting that the company expects to expand margins in 2026 through improvements in Medicare Advantage and commercial plans, which will offset the expected margin contraction in Medicaid.

Shares in UnitedHealth are down nearly 50% over the past year but rebounded some after Berkshire Hathaway revealed it had picked up about $1.57 billion in stock in the health insurer in the second quarter.

"We see this as not necessarily dissimilar from commentary in the second quarter; however, with the benefit of several more months of claims/trend progression, we think the incremental visibility underlines this positive commentary," the analysts wrote.

The ACA rate repricing may pose a more significant challenge for Centene Corp. (CNC), which has a disproportionately large number of members in the plans compared to its similarly sized competitors.

During a third quarter earnings call, Centene CEO Sarah London touted the benefits of extending ACA tax credits and that Centene believes "these tax credits offer critical support for hard-working Americans, small business owners and rural healthcare infrastructure."
